text.title: competitive-analysis Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for competitive-analysis. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Select 3-5 direct competitors (same problem, same user audience), 2-3 indirect competitors (same problem, different user or approach), and 1-2 aspirational examples (best-in-class UX from any industry). Cap total at 8 to maintain depth.
rule.rationale: More than 8 competitors transforms the analysis from depth-focused to breadth-focused. Breadth generates feature lists; depth generates insights. Direct competitors establish the baseline; indirect competitors reveal alternative approaches; aspirational examples break pattern lock.

rule.statement: Define competitor types before beginning: Direct (same problem, same user — e.g., Uber vs Lyft), Indirect (same problem, different approach — e.g., Uber vs public transit), Partial (overlapping features but different scope), Aspirational (best-in-class to learn from, not a direct competitor).
rule.rationale: Clarity on competitor type ensures the right analysis. Analyzing only direct competitors may miss solutions. Including aspirational examples exposes UX innovations from unrelated industries.

rule.statement: Establish evaluation criteria before examining any competitor product. Criteria derived after observation introduce confirmation bias.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc criteria selection is unconsciously biased: analysts weight criteria where their preferred design performs well. Pre-defined criteria are neutral and repeatable.

rule.statement: Use consistent scoring rubrics for all competitors. Prefer qualitative scales (Excellent/Good/Fair/Poor) over binary (Yes/No) to capture degree of implementation.
rule.rationale: Binary scoring loses nuance. A feature implemented poorly is not the same as a feature implemented well. Qualitative scales surface these degrees and produce better comparative insights.

rule.statement: Document your scoring criteria and definitions before beginning (e.g., "Excellent onboarding = completes in under 3 minutes and shows value immediately; Good = 3-5 minutes; Fair = 5-10 minutes; Poor = over 10 minutes or unclear value proposition").
rule.rationale: Without definitions, scoring drifts across competitors. Explicit definitions ensure consistency and make scores repeatable if someone else conducts the analysis.

rule.statement: Create real accounts and complete full user flows rather than reviewing screenshots or marketing pages. Use on both desktop and mobile, test edge cases and error states, contact support, test accessibility.
rule.rationale: Screenshots reveal visual design; actual use reveals friction. Error messages, loading states, support responsiveness, and onboarding flow are invisible in screenshots. These are often where competitors differentiate.

rule.statement: Conduct the analysis yourself (use the product, create accounts, complete full flows). Do not delegate deep qualitative pass to an agent or junior analyst.
rule.rationale: Deep insights come from hands-on use and noticing details that automated tools miss. Nuanced UX observations (tone, visual hierarchy, interaction smoothness) require human judgment.

rule.statement: Annotate screenshots and observations immediately. Memory of details degrades fast — hours later you will forget why a particular pattern was notable.
rule.rationale: Rich annotations during the session preserve context. Notes taken hours later lack the immediate sense of what made the interaction notable.

rule.statement: Use the same documentation structure for every competitor: per-competitor profile template with sections for overview, key screens (annotated), user flows (step-by-step), strengths, weaknesses, and opportunities.
rule.rationale: Structured templates ensure the same dimensions are evaluated for all competitors. Ad-hoc notes cannot be compared across products.

rule.statement: Separate "what they do" observations from "what we should do" recommendations. Keep them in distinct sections until synthesis. An observation ("Competitor A shows estimated delivery on the product page") is different from a recommendation ("we should show estimated delivery earlier").
rule.rationale: Mixing observations and recommendations conflates data with interpretation. Separate them to enable a later review where someone else can validate observations and challenge recommendations.

rule.statement: After documenting all competitors, identify patterns: what everyone does (baseline expectation), what nobody does (opportunity), what top performers do distinctively (best practice), and what problems appear in multiple products (anti-patterns).
rule.rationale: Individual competitor analysis is a data-gathering phase; pattern analysis is where data becomes insight. A feature present in all 5 competitors is table stakes; a feature present in 0 is an opportunity; a feature that top performers execute well and others botch is a best practice.

rule.statement: Flag patterns that differ by product type, user segment, or use case. A pattern that holds for enterprise SaaS may not hold for consumer products. Document these distinctions.
rule.rationale: Pattern blindness: assuming a pattern applies universally when it only applies to a specific segment. Noting segment differences prevents over-generalizing findings.

rule.statement: Include one aspirational non-competitor (different industry, best-in-class UX) to break pattern lock. Analyze whether patterns unique to your competitive set could be improved using patterns from outside your industry.
rule.rationale: Entire competitive sets can converge on suboptimal patterns. A pattern transfer from a different industry often yields the highest-value insights.

rule.statement: Categorize every recommendation into Must Have (table stakes), Should Have (parity), Could Differentiate (opportunity), or Avoid (failure mode).
rule.rationale: Without tiers, all recommendations appear equal and trigger debate about priority. Tiers make prioritization explicit: Must Have are blockers; Avoid are negative signals; Could Differentiate are the most valuable output.
